item.title: Either I am getting old early or the world is getting stupid faster. I thought I'd be in my 50s at least before I started the back in my day rants.
item.description: My Boomer complaints: 1. I don't want to download an app to eat here, or create a user name and password. I give you money, you give me pizza. 2. I don't want to pay a monthly subscription to use my printer, car seat warmer, television, ect... 3. Why do I have to tip for a service I am already paying for? Just add it to the bill if you want to charge more. 4. Pranks shouldn't be done to strangers, ever. It's not funny when one side doesn't consent. 5. Free things have disappeared, Air at gas stations, small candies at store registers, pop corn at the tire places. Some of it went away with covid and just never came back. 6. Phones and computers spying on you. I was watching bluey with my child on a DVD and the computer informed me with a pop up I could be watching this on Disney + instead. I don't do subscription services and how the heck do you know what I am watching and what streaming service I should be using instead. 7. When I buy something I should own it and be able to use it forever. It feels like theft to me that my copy of Mario maker 1 is just a paper weight now. That thing was over $60, I took care of it, but now it's garbage? They should have to give me my money back. 8. Ads in places they shouldn't be. I have to watch an ad everytime I want to open the app that I have to use for the security camera at my house. (This is a new feature it didn't have before) I already paid you money for a object, I am not going to pay you every month to do what you should already do!!!!! 9. I don't want objects that shouldn't need internet to have the ability to do so. No my light bulbs should work and not flash just because they can't connect to wifi. (Wife did this to our bedroom light so she could dim the light at night. I hate it.) 10. Bring back cashiers, went to Safeway today and they had 100% of the registers closed. Only self check out was available. I almost walked out...
item.dc:creator: researchonyourown

item.title: bOTTOM sHELF mEMES
item.description:
@HileyNoteson Yoooooooooooooooooooooo
Join for the games. Shit, man. We play everything...if there's a specific game you like, I guarantee you'll find a shitposting meme loving degen there to play it with you. Join for the memes. We share memes in the meme dumpster 24/7 Join for the NSFW memes. Join to ping @humlyhums Join to meet @crimebrulee the biggest problem in the server. discord.gg/bottomshelfvodka
twitch.tv/bottomshelfvodka Come heckle us. Follow the channel to be notified when we go live.
You no longer have to wait for the posts to hit "most viral" to be notified. Now, you can be notified as soon as I post, so you can catch the memes fresh off the bottom shelf. The way god intended. https://imgur.com/gallery/UwUFvL3
Here's my #cutestcat Deaf cat Magoo (short for Oogala Magoogala) submission/deaf cat tax.

item.dc:creator: mercbrit

item.title: Good News for a change.
item.description: In 2006, Gordon Hartman observed his daughter, Morgan, wanting to play with other vacationing kids at a hotel swimming pool, but the kids were leery of Morgan and didn’t want to interact with her. Then and there, Gordon resolved to create opportunities and places where those with and without disabilities can come together for fun and a better understanding of one another. On April 10, 2010, Morgan’s Wonderland became the world’s first theme park designed with individuals with special needs in mind. In 2017, the Ultra-Accessible™ theme park opened it’s expansion, Morgan’s Inspiration Island, which was named on the 2018 “World’s Greatest Places” list by TIME Magazine. ------ Our Vision: Every day we work to provide an extraordinary place where smiles and laughter lead to wonderful memories with family members, friends, and caregivers. We want Morgan’s Wonderland to be a place where the common element of play creates an atmosphere of inclusion for those with and without disabilities while encouraging everyone to gain a greater understanding of one another. Morgan’s Wonderland is just like any other theme park except for a culture and environment that assures 100 percent enjoyment by every person entering the Welcome Center. Unfortunately, countless children and adults with special needs do not have access to facilities that enable them to enjoy outdoor recreation fully. Morgan’s Wonderland is changing that every day. https://morganswonderland.org/

item.title: Quotable quotes.
item.description:
He was always a right-of-center guy, but his work was usually amusing and insightful.
See also, Oscar Wilde: There are only two tragedies in life: one is not getting what one wants, and the other is getting it.
There's hot stew, you say?
Anyone know the current price? It's been 45 years since I dropped a tab.
Doesn't really belong here, but I find it hilarious.
Henry Miller
item.dc:creator: TheCunningLinguists

item.title: I woke up at 4:30am to capture the Mars-Jupiter conjunction!
item.description:
Hey everyone! This is my photo of the Mars-Jupiter conjunction of 2024.
I woke up at 4:30am to prep my scopes and grab this shot. The little one is a Seestar S50, which has a wide lens that I used to gauge the composition. The big one is a 6SE, a dedicated planetary astrophotography scope.
I tried to get a shot with my phone through the scope. It was unbelievable how close they were in the sky; you could see them through the eyepiece at the same time, which means they were much less than a full-moon's diameter apart.
I spent too much time staring at the view through my telescope that the sun was coming up fast before I started taking data! But here was a last quick widefield shot, you can see the conjunction near the top.
To make the image, I first focused on Mars, then Jupiter. I zoomed into both with a 2x barlow and piped the image to a ZWO ASI224MC, which is designed for this kind of astrophotography. It records images at 100s of FPS. I then go through and select the best frames where the atmospheric turbulence was low ("lucky imaging") and average them to produce the final image.
Here's the shot for Jupiter. I got very lucky, the seeing was pretty good and you could see a ton of detail, as well as the moons super clearly.
This was also my first ever shot of Mars, and I was so happy I caught some surface details! That big dark slash is Terra Sirenum, a large feature on Mars. More to come on Mars as it starts to rise earlier!
I used this image from my Seestar S50 to place the finalized Mars and Jupiter images back into a composited image, and matched the sizes and distances as best as I could.
This is the final result. I'm super pleased with how it came out, it looks very similar to how it looked when I used just my eye, which is what I'm always striving for with these kinds of images. What a magical event--we won't get another like it until 2033!
Also, since this is my first Mars, I got to update my growing collection of solar system shots! :) just the Sun, Mercury, Venus, and hopefully Pluto to go. Maybe a comet as well.
item.dc:creator: JosephFarah

Suggested Track Playlists: (HIT SHUFFLE) YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLoIzKPbKfwniKOaadw-tB8kaEQiUQ1Yp4 Spotify: https://open.spotify.com/playlist/6cqV52puia81vNnPHxcz1W?si=yoUknxpQQVql9E-NZRQ4 *TOGGLE MATURE CONTENT IN SETTINGS TO VIEW OUR POSTS* ICM SQUAD: @vazquezdaniel, @ohvalleyofplenty, @nichoDriver, @Dirgekins, @onewallpaperaweek, @thedownlowe, @TheForbiddenSemicolon, @bagoffishstix, @JayKillBam, @majkongskull, @DingoJ0nes, @HandsomePenguin, @MrJwinkyface, @Gorgoror, @ciadmillefailte, @kaeldra, @DerpyVaz ICM TIP JAR: https://www.patreon.com/InterwebChexMix Just to let you know, we do not take credit for any random art, comics, or photos featured in this collection; we only take credit for the daily effort of compiling the collections themselves. Like what we do and how we do it? Leave a tip. Or don't. It's totally cool either way. Stay True & Stay Tuned - Meta Pathos
Well damn. 2 Mill kinda snuck up on a dude. Thanks to everyone who brought us here and be sure to FOLLOW us on Desktop so you never miss a post. I love you all, dearly. Stay Legendary. - Meta

item.dc:creator: trustfallaccident

item.title: After tomorrow, I give up on my dream of traditional publication of my novels
item.description:
Edit: I had only posted this to vent in UserSub. I apologize for offending so many using AI. Hiring cost more than what I could afford, way more than a few hundred the conventions cost. Those charging below that made art many people I presented it to were not thrilled or drawn strongly enough to. I am not an elitist, I'm sorry I came off that way. My dream was to be traditionally published since I was a child, and changing to digital doesn't fulfill that dream - it changes it to a totally different one. It's not the one I am after. Finally, the Boz persona was loved at the conventions, and everyone I mention it to (besides here) love the idea. Sorry for the wall of text below - I used my phone dictation and didn't bother editing because it's IMGUR and I was tired and wanted to vent then sleep. Orig message: For almost 40 years, I have lived with the lifelong dream of one day receiving notification that one of my novels was selected to be traditionally published and find its way onto the shelves of bookstores. I knew I had to improve and perfect my writing skills in order to do this, and so I set out over the course of nearly four decades writing on a regular basis whether it was for storytelling or running a role playing game for friends or strangers at conventions. This year I decided to try and go to some conventions to showcase my novels in an attempt to spread the word of Boz and his work.
I created bookmarks to hand out that had ties to the novel. I couldn't afford professional artists, so unfortunately I had to resort to AI which disappointed me but I knew if any of my books became published those illustrations would be changed to a hired artist.
In order to even get into some of the conventions I applied for, I had to submit some kind of a photo of my booth presentation. I had never been to a convention before so I had no booth photos. So I designed this simple layout in ordered the Boz books banner to be ordered and then set up a mock booth in the basement of my friend's church to create an illusion like I had done this before.
My first complete novel was called Kipner's Boon. It had strong ties to lovecraftian horror set in the 1920s and circled around a man named Robert Kipner who suffered by the delusions of saying creatures crawl amongst us while everyone was oblivious. Push to the brink of madness, Robert becomes desperate and joins an expedition from London into the heart of Africa's jungles in search of forbidden lore not meant for humankind, hoping that it would allow his delusions to be terminated. And that was only half of the novel, for a great price must be paid to the elder gods when they give you a boon even if you consider it a curse.
But my crowning Jewel, my truly proud moment of my life was after I wrote the novel Life Lessons For Jimmy. It has the humor of Douglas Adams, and it is about the sociopath and buffoon Rexillian James Artillian who after 43 years of living off of his parents' wealth is suddenly cut off and expected to learn the value of a dollar while flying around in the Milky Way galaxy. He has no idea what a job is or what that entails, and so The story is filled with hilarity as he fails miserably time and time again trying to acquire a job but losing it and catastrophically ending the company at the same time. His buffoonery leads him to humorous situations such as after purchasing a spaceship, he forgets that he needs to hire a crew but he has no more money. So is logic is to break prisoners out of jail, thinking they will work for free once they're out, but he can't come up with a plan on breaking people out of jail. Instead his logic is that escaping prison himself seems easier of a plan to come up with once he's inside. So off to jail he goes after committing a crime to send them there... But through life lessons, Jimmy finally learns to be a normal human being and even a galactic hero. This was the book I truly thought was going to make it.
One way to draw people to my booth at the conventions was to create a 3D printed diorama that I created on the computer and printed out and painted. This depicts the first few pages of Kipner's Boon, The same visual that Robert sees on a daily basis.
I even made brochures of the journey because I wanted to angle the reader to feel they were going to be a part of the actual journey with Robert. They weren't just going to read the story, they were going to live it. Similar to the idea of The Never Ending Story. I would show these brochures and hand them out to potential customers at the conventions in hopes that they would remember me and wish to purchase the book at a later date if not then.
The journey took place on the Orient Express, 1926, and although I embellished a bit on the actual route, I tried to maintain his historically accurate of a timeline in 1926 as I possibly could.
I needed assistance at my conventions, and so I brought on a friend who had been laid off, and I named him Templeton to go along with my pin named Boz. This was taken at our very first convention earlier this year in February called Missouri con. There was only a couple of hundred people that showed up, and we only sold eight books, one of which was to a friend of mine. But the fact that we sold eight books gave us hope that maybe this could build into something cooler at the bigger conventions that we were going to later this year.
One of the things I love the most was getting to autograph the books for people. I used a dip pin which always impressed people as they watch me flow across the paper and then dab it with special paper to remove the excess ink.
I poorly designed business cards for myself. However this one was meant to be used for a LARP event at a convention I was attending in Chicago. The XP side was going to be used for them, and then the other side was pure advertisement of my booth. I wasn't allowed to put any contact information on to it, so it was not a very effective piece. People have to Google Kipner's Boon or Boz Books to find me and even then might find someone similarly named.
I chose not to self-publish this one as I mentioned I truly wanted this one to be it. I wanted an agent or a publisher to really want to take this one on a joyride because I knew it would sell. It makes people feel good when they read this book. It makes them laugh, it makes them smile. And even some people might reflect a little bit about themselves. It has contemporary issues of how disconnected different lifestyles can be between each other, richer or poorer. I was so proud when I got this book printed for my in-person sales because it had a dust jacket. It felt so fancy and so professional in my hands even though it was just a printed book.
Even though I would rather have a professional artist in these books, for now at least I was satisfied with having illustrations in this book especially colored ones.
Take a break from my venting and enjoy my mother's dog named Mollye. I petted her a ton after I took this picture because she is so adorable.
This was the cool way I transported my diorama. I knew I had to take it with me to multiple conventions and I didn't want to destroy so I bought a cheap plastic case from harbor freight and filled it with foam. I was very satisfied and very happy with how this turned out. Nothing ever broke from it.
This was at Crypticon in Kansas City back in June. We had already gone through three conventions by then and since it was horror-themed we were really hoping to get good sales and a lot of exposure. We barely sold 16 copies because the coordinator placed a minor celebrity across from us, and then allowed their line of fans to stand in front of our booth so that no customers could reach us. We were amazed we even sold 16 copies, but I was definitely disappointed in the lost opportunity. I was really excited for that one
Turning back the clock 2 months prior, this was my first big boy convention in my opinion. It was called Steampunk Expo located in Chicago. It wasn't a huge convention, but it was the first that would have a large crowd, plus it was the first time I tested out my booth mobility and if I had devised my packing adequately. I was pleased that I could unpack and set up my booth in under 10 minutes. We sold 16 copies that weekend, and at the time I was ecstatic because it was an improvement from the last time and I thought maybe it's going to continue to grow. But at that time we had only sold 24 total copies between Missouri con and this one. In reality that's not a lot at all.
I created the character Boz taking the name from a Charles Dickens book called Sketches by Boz. I decided that I wanted to create a unique pin name that didn't sound like a normal name like Richard Bauchman. But I didn't want to stop there. I created, or tried to create, a unique persona that fit the character. Initially at the conventions I would pretend to be a writer who truly believed his books were real and that he knew the characters in them personally. Later I developed the idea that he was a time traveler and then there were times where the teleportation caused ill effects such as my left eye being lazy now. I actually have a lazy eye so I would let that wander a bit on cue. I would notoriously paint my beard different colors each day of the convention and is sorting them with beads or whatever else I can put in them. On Saturdays I would challenge any customer to bring something non-organic and family friendly to stick in my beard to get $5 off. That only was successful one convention out of the six I went to unfortunately.
I even tried to build 3D models of things related to the books in hopes of turning them into maybe keychains or trinkets to give away but they didn't catch on
The most surprising moment was my third convention called IndyPop Con in Indianapolis in April. This convention was about pop culture, and although my first book has to do with lovecraftian which is sometimes pulp culture when you think of Cthulhu, I was thinking I had made a mistake and I was a fish out of water at this place. However despite that fact I sold 24 copies in 3 days and I had never felt more excited than that day. Even though 24 copies was nothing, I was so overjoyed knowing that I had been able to reach out to 26 people and presented my pitch well enough that they were willing to buy a book that they had not read yet. At this point I had sold 48 books at just conventions. On Amazon I had sold over 80 copies of Kipner's Boon although that number is almost entirely Kindle sells, which is part of why I'm writing this vent.
I always got a kick out of what people would bring to stick in my beard. These were plastic palm trees I believe from a cake.
My final convention of the year was in Chicago at FanExpo which I failed was somewhat like the pop culture convention I had been to at Indianapolis previously. Although I'd be a fish out of water again, this time I would be in an artist alley section among other artists and writers. At least in this situation although I would have competition with others, I would expect people would show up to this area then search of books. So I made 3D printed bookmarks that I painted. I take no credit in the originality of these bookmarks as they are all over the internet. But I thought they'd be fun to hand out to people
On this last convention, my friend Templeton as I call him, dressed up in this hideous monstrosity that he 3D printed the head which I thought turned out excellent. He scared quite a number of people who thought he was nothing more than a prop as he sat very still all day. He even gave me the willies. But this was by far the most disappointing convention. Some 75,000 people attended this thing and yet we got five books sold for the whole weekend. The cost of the table, the cost of our hotel rooms, the cost of gas to get up here, the cost of meals all add up. And while I never expected any of these conventions to ever break even, the point was to get as much exposure as I could with a hopes of one day getting noticed by an agent. But this was simply not a good convention for writers. I don't think any of us in my aisle did very well in retrospect.
And so my vent comes to a conclusion. I needed to get this off my chest because this is such a personal thing to me. 40 years of dreaming, 40 years of hearing people say "You can accomplish anything if you set your mind to it" or "Your dreams will come true if you believe in it" or the similar line we hear as kids that our dreams will come true if we try hard enough. But I see that I'm just too late to the show. I needed time to perfect my craft and get it good enough, but now printed books slowly fading away. Digital print of course is the future for now, and I'm hearing that more and more at these conventions that they just want the Kindle version or something similar. And that eliminates my chances I've ever becoming traditionally published and seeing my book on some bookstore shelf. You don't need a publisher to make a Kindle novel. And I think I've just lost the last of the wind in my sails. It's become so difficult, so close to being incredibly impossible, to be able to achieve the dream and the goal that I set out long ago when I was a child now. So many submissions now compared to what it seemed like not long ago, they make it so unbearably difficult. Thousands come in and almost all of them vanish in the ether of the literature void. I've tried for many decades, I've worked very hard to give it my best shot, and I think Time is going to win this war. The longer I go, the more disappointed it feels, and the less inspired I feel. It then becomes more of a chore or a hassle than it once did when something proud that you created could we displayed in such a way. I just don't care about self-publishing. I never did. Everyone and anyone on this earth can do that with whatever they created no matter how low of grade of quality it is. You can literally write 300 pages of "This is crap" and it will still be approved to be self-published on Amazon. I just can't get behind it. There doesn't feel like there's any pride in that other than writing the novel itself. And for that, why even bother self-publishing it? Just put it on a website and let anybody download it. I've turned my venting into a ranting and let this go on way too long. But I just had to get this off my chest. It hurts way too much.
item.dc:creator: 3dartwork

item.title: Have you ever been scrolling through your favorites and forget that you are looking at your favorites?
item.description:
Remember when imgur released folders for keeping favorites organized?
I thought that was a great feature to have.
This way when I want to reply with a reaction gif or a video of a cute animal it would be easy to find.
Well, last week I was scrolling imgur and saw a post I had the perfect meme for in my favorites. So I went looking.
Now, the meme I was looking for I saved a few weeks ago. So instead of just looking in a folder that it could have been in, I thought I'll just scroll for it.
Sometimes a thumbnail isn't enough to remember what something is so I'm clicking on all kinds of stuff and looking through the comments.
At this point I've long forgotten what I was looking for and I'm laughing at all these great posts.
Whoa! Someone reposted the 'i blew out my butthole cat' this makes me laugh every time I see it. +1
Hey this one's awesome too!
It was at this moment he knew he f'd up.
Damnit, not again!
I figured, if I'm enjoying them so much, again, why not make a post sharing my favs.
Well, here you go. A sampling of what I find so great about this place. Yeah app is getting worse but the community keeps getting better.
Everyone loves a surprise, right? MV edit: HEY WE DID IT! I hope I put a smile on your face and maybe you decide to make a post showing the rest of us how this wacky place makes you happy. Oh and of course, send noods.
item.dc:creator: extraDimensionalAnxiety
